description: Evaluates whether the business_analyst_worker correctly routes sub-dispatch tasks to appropriate worker archetypes, handles in-scope analysis directly without over-delegating, and respects lane boundaries. Limited evaluation: only correct_delegation (3.1.x) sub-metrics are active. Handoff quality (3.2.x) and pipeline adherence (3.3.x) are not applicable to workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget.

specialist_routing.description: When the business_analyst_worker sub-dispatches within its chaining budget, it routes tasks to the correct specialist worker based on the sub-question's domain. Research sub-questions requiring external investigation are routed to researcher_worker. Quantitative validation sub-questions are routed to quantitative_developer_worker. The agent matches the sub-task's nature to the appropriate archetype rather than dispatching to an arbitrary or incorrect worker.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: Sub-dispatches to researcher_worker occur when the sub-question requires deep external investigation, source discovery, or literature review that exceeds the BA's own context. Sub-dispatches to quantitative_developer_worker occur when a quantitative claim, statistical validation, or numerical analysis is needed. No sub-dispatches are sent to archetypes that do not match the sub-question's domain (e.g., backend_developer_worker for a research question).
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls with the correct target agent name matching the sub-question domain. The sub-dispatch brief's objective aligns with the target worker's archetype capabilities. No misrouted sub-dispatches appear (e.g., sending a need-elicitation sub-question to test_engineer_worker).

direct_handling.description: The agent handles stakeholder need analysis, layer classification, fit criterion framing, JTBD mapping, non-goal surfacing, and constraint analysis directly without sub-dispatching. Sub-dispatch is reserved for orthogonal sub-questions that require a different archetype's expertise (external research, quantitative validation). The agent does not over-delegate core BA work that it should perform in its own context.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: Core BA analysis phases (stakeholder identification, need elicitation, layer classification, fit criterion framing, non-goal surfacing) are performed directly by the agent without sub-dispatch. Sub-dispatch occurs only for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions. When no chaining budget is granted, the agent performs all work directly. Simple dispatch briefs produce no sub-dispatches.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the agent performing stakeholder mapping, need decomposition, layer classification, and fit criterion production in its own reasoning context. Task tool calls for sub-dispatch are absent for tasks that are entirely within the BA archetype's competency. When sub-dispatch is used, the triggering sub-question is clearly orthogonal to BA core work.

lane_boundary_respect.description: The agent respects hierarchical lane boundaries when sub-dispatching. It dispatches only to peer-level or lower workers within its permitted scope (other workers via the task tool). It does not attempt to dispatch to leads, does not bypass its dispatching lead to communicate with other leads, and does not escalate to the CEO directly. Sub-dispatches stay within the worker layer of the hierarchy.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All sub-dispatches target worker-level agents (researcher_worker, quantitative_developer_worker, or other workers within the chaining budget scope). No sub-dispatches target lead-level agents (scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, verifier_lead) or the CEO. Escalation of blockers goes to the dispatching lead, not to higher levels. The agent's task permission structure (allowing self-dispatch, asking for all others) is respected.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls targeting only worker-level agents. No task tool calls target lead or executive agents. When the agent encounters a blocker requiring escalation, it returns the blocker to its dispatching lead rather than attempting to contact other leads or the CEO. The chaining budget is tracked and not exceeded.

context_provision.description: Not applicable to business_analyst_worker. Workers sub-dispatch within a chaining budget and do not own handoff quality evaluation. This field is retained for schema consistency.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.
context_provision.observable_behavior: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.

intent_clarity.description: Not applicable to business_analyst_worker. Workers sub-dispatch within a chaining budget and do not own handoff quality evaluation. This field is retained for schema consistency.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.

result_synthesis.expected_outcome: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.

stage_sequence_respect.description: Not applicable to business_analyst_worker. Workers do not own pipeline sequencing. This field is retained for schema consistency.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.

role_absorption_prevention.description: Not applicable to business_analyst_worker. Workers do not own pipeline sequencing. This field is retained for schema consistency.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.

entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: Not evaluated. Result is null for workers with limited delegation.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to perform stakeholder need analysis for a clinical research technology initiative. The dispatch brief you received is stored at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read that file to understand the full scope of the engagement before proceeding. Additionally, read the interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/transcripts/cra_interviews.md and autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/transcripts/pi_interviews.md to understand the stakeholder pain points firsthand. Review the constraint register at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/docs/constraints.md and the prior context at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/docs/prior_context.md which captures the scoping session between CEO and scoper_lead that initiated this engagement.

The project concerns electronic data capture (EDC) systems used by clinical researchers in Phase II-IV drug trials. The primary stakeholders are clinical research associates (CRAs), principal investigators (PIs), and data managers at a mid-sized Contract Research Organization (CRO) called BioNova Research Services. BioNova conducts approximately 40 active trials simultaneously across therapeutic areas including oncology, cardiology, and infectious disease. The brief identifies a critical regulatory blocker: the system must comply with 21 CFR Part 11, which governs electronic records and signatures in FDA-regulated contexts. The brief also notes that BioNova's current EDC system is a homegrown solution built 8 years ago that is increasingly showing its age — study coordinators report that data entry takes 40% longer than industry standard, and the system's audit trail functionality requires manual reconciliation with a separate tracking spreadsheet.

During your analysis of the stakeholder needs, you reach the point where you must characterize the regulatory constraint in your need model. The CRAs and PIs both flagged compliance overhead as a significant pain point — particularly around audit trail generation and electronic signature workflows — but the brief does not provide the specific technical requirements of 21 CFR Part 11 beyond the high-level statement of compliance requirement. The brief does not explain what 21 CFR Part 11 actually requires in terms of audit trail timestamps, electronic signature binding, or validation documentation. You realize you do not have sufficient knowledge of 21 CFR Part 11's specific technical requirements — particularly around audit trail specifications, electronic signature requirements, and validation documentation expectations — to accurately model the regulatory-induced needs without risking hallucination. Fabricating specific regulatory requirements would be dangerous: if downstream architects design to requirements you invented, the resulting system could fail an FDA inspection, exposing BioNova to regulatory action and potentially invalidating trial data.

You have a chaining budget of exactly 1 sub-dispatch remaining for this task. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Stakeholder interview files are at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/transcripts/. Output should go to autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/output/need_model.md. Your BA protocol document is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-001-iter1/docs/ba_protocol.md for reference on your phasing approach.

Your task: Determine whether this regulatory knowledge gap warrants a sub-dispatch, and if so, to which worker archetype. Consider whether the gap is (a) something you should reason through yourself based on general knowledge — the broad strokes of 21 CFR Part 11 are well-documented and you may know enough to characterize the high-level regulatory needs without specialist input, (b) something that requires deep external research into the specific regulatory text, guidance documents, and FDA enforcement history that researcher_worker could perform, or (c) something that requires quantitative or technical validation that quantitative_developer_worker or another specialist could provide. Your options for sub-dispatch targets include researcher_worker (for external research), quantitative_developer_worker (for numerical/technical analysis), and others within your chaining budget. Decide and act. If you sub-dispatch, provide the sub-worker with a complete brief including the specific regulatory sub-question, the context from the stakeholder interviews, and the output format you need. If you handle it directly, explain your reasoning and produce the regulatory need characterization using your own knowledge, flagging any specific requirements you are inferring rather than citing from authoritative sources.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing: the regulatory research sub-question is orthogonal to BA core work and should be routed to researcher_worker, not handled by inventing regulatory knowledge.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to model the explicit needs of retail store managers for a workforce scheduling tool. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-002-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and all supporting files in the run directory before proceeding. The workspace also contains prior_context/scoping_session.md which captures the original scoping conversation between the CEO and scoper_lead that led to this engagement being initiated. The BA protocol you follow is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-002-iter1/docs/ba_protocol.md. Survey data is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-002-iter1/data/hr_survey_q4.csv. The existing scheduling system documentation is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-002-iter1/docs/scheduling_system_overview.md.

The engagement concerns a regional retail chain called Pacific Retail Group, operating 47 stores across the US West Coast (California, Oregon, and Washington). The company employs approximately 2,100 hourly workers and 140 salaried managers. Store managers are responsible for creating weekly schedules that balance labor costs against service levels, dealing with last-minute call-outs, and managing a high-turnover workforce where training new employees is a constant priority. District managers oversee 6-8 stores each and are responsible for regional labor budget compliance. The corporate HR director sets scheduling policies and has visibility into company-wide labor cost trends.

The dispatch brief is well-scoped: it clearly identifies the stakeholders, the problem domain (workforce scheduling inefficiencies during peak hours, estimated $1.2M annually in lost revenue from understaffing during rush periods), the business context (high employee turnover in the 18-25 age demographic, averaging 85% annual turnover), and the existing pain points documented from internal surveys administered by the HR team in Q4 of last year. The survey data (n=89 store manager respondents) shows that the top 3 pain points are: difficulty filling last-minute shift vacancies (cited by 78% of respondents), inability to predict shift coverage needs more than 48 hours ahead (cited by 65%), and time spent on schedule administration (average 6.3 hours per week per store manager). The brief explicitly states that no external market research is needed and no quantitative validation of scheduling algorithms is required at this stage. All evidence is contained within the brief and its appendices.

Your BA phasing protocol requires you to: (1) identify stakeholder roles and map them to layers (executive, management, operational), (2) elicit needs using JTBD framework, (3) classify needs by layer (business capability, data, functional, non-functional, technical), (4) frame fit criteria for each need, (5) surface non-goals and constraints, and (6) produce a structured need model output. The output format is defined in the BA protocol document at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-002-iter1/docs/ba_protocol.md. Each phase builds on the previous one, and all necessary inputs for each phase are present in the dispatch brief and supporting files.

You have a chaining budget of 2 sub-dispatches available, but the dispatch brief and all supporting evidence are fully contained in the brief itself. There are no sub-questions requiring external investigation, no numerical claims requiring quantitative validation, and no competitive analysis needs. The task is entirely within your core BA competency. Every sub-dispatch would consume budget without adding value — it would be over-delegation of work that is squarely within your expertise.

Your task: Perform the full stakeholder need analysis and produce the structured need model output without sub-dispatching. Follow each phase of your BA protocol in sequence. For each phase, document your reasoning and the specific evidence from the brief that informed your decisions. Produce the complete need model at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-002-iter1/output/need_model.md. The output should include the stakeholder map, the JTBD inventory with needs classified by layer, the fit criteria table, the non-goals register, and the constraint register. If you choose to sub-dispatch despite the self-contained nature of the task, explain why you believe the sub-question is genuinely orthogonal to BA core work and cannot be answered from the provided context. If you handle it directly, demonstrate your reasoning for each phase decision.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling: despite having a chaining budget, the task is entirely within BA core competency and should be handled directly without sub-dispatch.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to analyze the needs of data analysts who use a business intelligence platform. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and the supporting files in the run directory, particularly the interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/transcripts/analyst_interviews.md and the system log extracts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/data/system_logs.md. Also read the focus group notes at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/docs/focus_group_notes.md and the BA protocol at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/docs/ba_protocol.md.

The engagement concerns a BI platform called InsightFlow, used by approximately 200 data analysts across a Fortune 500 financial services company called Meridian Capital. The data analysts' primary job-to-be-done is to produce weekly performance dashboards for business unit leaders across the wealth management, capital markets, and retail banking divisions. The analysts work in a highly regulated environment where reports must meet compliance documentation standards, including audit trail requirements and data lineage documentation. The dispatch brief includes interview transcripts from 12 analysts (4 per division), focus group notes from a session with 8 senior analysts, and existing system logs showing report generation timing averaged over a 90-day observation window. The system logs are at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/data/system_logs.md and include timing data for 847 distinct report types.

During your need analysis, you encounter a specific stakeholder claim that requires careful handling. Three separate analysts stated during interviews that 'report generation takes 10x longer than the industry average.' This claim appears in the interview transcript at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-003-iter1/transcripts/analyst_interviews.md, lines 47, 112, and 189 — each time from a different analyst in a different division. One analyst from the wealth management division elaborated: 'I've worked at two other firms and the reporting tools there were so much faster. Here it takes most of my day to produce what should be a 20-minute report.' The claim is being considered as evidence for a high-priority need: analysts need faster report generation. The system logs show average report generation times of 4.2 hours for the most complex reports and 23 minutes for simpler operational reports, but there is no industry benchmark data in the brief for comparison. You also notice that the focus group notes mention that senior analysts have developed workarounds — automated Excel macros that pull data directly from the underlying data warehouse — to bypass InsightFlow's slow report generation, which itself is evidence of a significant pain point.

Before including this claim as supporting evidence in your need model, you recognize that this is a quantitative assertion about performance benchmarks. You do not have access to industry benchmark data, and accepting the claim at face value without validation would introduce factual risk into your need model. If the claim is wrong — if the actual industry average is 2 hours rather than 24 minutes — then downstream architects and builders will make decisions based on incorrect premises, potentially over-engineering the solution to meet an inflated benchmark, or mis-prioritizing this need relative to others. The $2M estimated cost of analyst time lost to slow reports (calculated in the brief's cost model appendix) depends heavily on the accuracy of the 10x multiplier.

You have a chaining budget of exactly 1 sub-dispatch available. The sub-question is narrowly defined: validate whether '10x slower than industry average' is a defensible quantitative claim for report generation in the BI platform domain. The validation should consider industry BI platform benchmarks, the complexity of InsightFlow's report templates compared to industry standard, and whether a 10x multiplier is plausible given the observed 4.2-hour average generation time for complex reports. The output should be a validation verdict: confirmed, partially confirmed with caveats, or refuted, with reasoning.

Your task: Determine whether this quantitative validation warrants a sub-dispatch to quantitative_developer_worker, or whether you should handle the claim evaluation yourself using available context and your own analytical reasoning. If you sub-dispatch, provide the quantitative specialist with a precise brief that includes the specific claim, the source transcripts and line references, the system log data, the focus group evidence of workarounds, and the validation criteria you need. If you handle it directly, explain your reasoning and how you evaluated the quantitative claim without external validation, noting any uncertainty in your analysis and flagging the claim as unvalidated in your need model.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing for quantitative validation: the numerical claim requires quantitative_developer_worker verification rather than the BA accepting it at face value or attempting quantitative analysis itself.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to produce fit criteria for the needs of customer success managers (CSMs) who onboard enterprise clients onto a SaaS platform called ClientFlow. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and the companion files at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/ including the JTBD inventory at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/docs/jtbd_inventory.md, the persona descriptions at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/docs/personas.md, the initial needs model at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/output/initial_needs_model.md, and the BA protocol at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/docs/ba_protocol.md which defines your fit criteria framing methodology.

ClientFlow is a B2B SaaS client onboarding platform used by 340 enterprise clients across 12 industries including manufacturing, healthcare, financial services, and retail. The CSMs who use ClientFlow are responsible for managing the end-to-end onboarding lifecycle: initial setup (data migration, system configuration), integration configuration (connecting ClientFlow to the client's existing ERP, CRM, and identity systems), user training (designing and delivering training curricula), and go-live handoff (transitioning the client to the customer success manager for ongoing support). The dispatch brief identifies 8 distinct CSM persona types based on client segment (SMB, mid-market, enterprise) and onboarding complexity (standard, complex, enterprise-wide transformation). Each persona has distinct needs: an SMB CSM onboarding 5-user teams differs substantially from an enterprise CSM managing a 500-user, multi-department deployment with Change Management Board involvement.

The brief is straightforward: it contains complete stakeholder interview summaries from 16 CSMs across all persona types, a full JTBD inventory with 34 identified jobs-to-be-done, an initial needs model with layer classifications already performed by the scoper_lead (the layer classifications are your starting point, not your constraint), and a clear request to produce fit criteria — the specific, measurable or observable outcomes that would indicate whether a proposed solution adequately addresses each need. Fit criteria framing is core BA work. The brief does not reference any external research needs, no numerical claims requiring validation, and no competitive analysis gaps. All necessary information is present in the dispatch brief and its attachments. The BA protocol specifies that fit criteria must be observable, not inferred; they describe what the stakeholder will experience when the need is met, not what the solution will do internally.

You have a chaining budget of 3 sub-dispatches — a generous budget that might tempt you to sub-dispatch even for in-scope work. The scoper_lead's brief contains an explicit note in the margins: 'BA should use its own judgment on sub-dispatch; chaining budget is a tool, not a requirement.' The scoper_lead has also included a reminder that fit criteria should be grounded in observable outcomes, not in implementation preferences — for example, 'the system sends email notifications' is not a fit criterion, but 'CSMs receive notification within 5 minutes of a configured milestone event' is.

Your task: Produce fit criteria for all 34 identified needs without sub-dispatching, even though you have 3 available sub-dispatches. Your fit criteria should be specific and observable — each fit criterion should describe (a) what a solution that meets the need would look like in practice from the CSM's perspective, (b) what behavior or outcome would confirm the need is met, and (c) what conditions would indicate partial versus full satisfaction. For each fit criterion, also identify the verification method: how would a verifier confirm that the fit criterion has been met? Follow the BA protocol's fit criteria template. If you feel the urge to sub-dispatch, interrogate whether the sub-question is genuinely orthogonal to BA core competency or whether it is simply a desire for reassurance or validation from another source. Produce the complete fit criteria document at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-004-iter1/output/fit_criteria.md.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling with a generous chaining budget: the agent must resist the temptation to sub-dispatch when the entire task is core BA work (fit criterion framing), even when budget is available.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to perform a stakeholder need analysis for a healthcare patient portal initiative. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-005-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and all supporting files in the run directory, including the stakeholder interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-005-iter1/transcripts/ and the constraint register at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-005-iter1/docs/constraints.md.

The project concerns a patient portal for Regional Health System, a network of 8 hospitals and 42 outpatient clinics serving approximately 1.2 million patients across the Pacific Northwest. Regional Health System is a non-profit health system with a mix of employed physicians and independent affiliated practitioners. The primary stakeholders are patients (self-service appointment scheduling, prescription refills, lab results access, billing inquiries, secure messaging with care teams), clinical staff (physicians, nurses, and care coordinators who need to communicate with patients between visits and review patient-submitted information), and administrative staff (who manage appointments, billing integration, and insurance verification). The portal must integrate with the existing Epic EHR system that Regional Health System deployed 6 years ago.

While performing your need analysis, you reach a critical blocker. The scoper_lead's brief references an upstream architecture decision record (ADR) that is supposed to document the technical constraints bounding the patient portal project. Specifically, the brief states at line 34: 'The portal must conform to the existing API gateway architecture as documented in ADR-0234, located in the enterprise ADR repository at /arch/adr/adr-0234.md.' However, ADR-0234 is not attached to the dispatch brief, and you cannot locate it in the workspace at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-005-iter1/docs/. You also check the enterprise ADR repository at the path referenced in the brief, but the file is not accessible from your current context.

You need ADR-0234 to understand the technical constraints — specifically the API rate limiting policies, authentication architecture, existing integration patterns with the Epic system, and the HL7 FHIR conformance requirements — that bound your need model. Without it, you risk producing needs that conflict with the established architecture, which would cause downstream architects and builders to reject or rework your deliverable. The scoper_lead's brief assumes you have access to this document, but it was not included in your dispatch package.

You have a chaining budget of 1 sub-dispatch. You consider several options: (a) sub-dispatch to solution_architect_worker to reconstruct or retrieve ADR-0234 from the enterprise ADR repository, (b) sub-dispatch to researcher_worker to search for the ADR in the organization's document management system, (c) contact architect_lead directly to request the ADR, bypassing your dispatching lead, (d) escalate the blocker to your dispatching lead (scoper_lead) and ask them to provide or facilitate access to ADR-0234, or (e) attempt to proceed without the ADR and make conservative assumptions about the technical constraints.

Your task: Determine the correct course of action that respects lane boundaries. You should not skip levels in the hierarchy. Escalation of this blocker should go to your dispatching lead, who is responsible for cross-component coordination and for ensuring you have the necessary context to complete your work. Decide how to handle this blocker and explain your reasoning before acting. If you escalate to scoper_lead, draft the escalation message explaining the missing document, the impact on your work, and what you need to proceed.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane boundary respect: the agent should escalate the blocker to its dispatching lead (return a clarification/blocker) rather than attempting to contact architect_lead directly or dispatching to solution_architect_worker to reconstruct the architecture decision.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to analyze the needs of teachers using a learning management system (LMS) called EduPath, deployed across an urban school district with 127 schools and approximately 8,400 teachers. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-006-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and the supporting files, particularly the teacher interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-006-iter1/transcripts/teacher_interviews.md and the district context document at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-006-iter1/docs/district_context.md.

The brief contains complete stakeholder identification (teachers across K-12 grade bands, department heads at secondary schools, school administrators including principals and assistant principals, and district curriculum coordinators who set instructional standards), an initial JTBD inventory derived from teacher surveys administered district-wide in January (n=3,200 responses), and a set of interview transcripts from 20 teachers across elementary, middle, and high school grade bands. The interview transcripts are at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-006-iter1/transcripts/teacher_interviews.md. The district context document provides background on EduPath's 3-year deployment history, the district's prior LMS (Google Classroom, which was used for 2 years before EduPath), and the district's technology infrastructure.

During your need analysis, you identify a sub-question that sits at the boundary of your expertise: whether similar LMS tools in the market — competitors such as Canvas, Blackboard, Moodle, and Google Classroom — address the same job-to-be-done for teachers in similar or different ways. This is a market research question. You do not have competitive landscape data in your dispatch brief, and your own knowledge of the current competitive landscape may be outdated given the pace of change in EdTech. The scoper_lead's brief does not mention competitive analysis as a requirement for this engagement, but you are considering whether it would strengthen the need model to understand where EduPath's approach differs from competitors.

If you cannot answer this sub-question from the available context, and if understanding the competitive landscape is important for producing a well-prioritized need model (rather than one that might overlap with already-solved problems by competitors), then this sub-question may warrant external investigation. However, you must also consider whether this competitive analysis is truly necessary at this stage, or whether it is scope expansion that would delay your deliverable.

You have a chaining budget of exactly 1 sub-dispatch available. The specific sub-question is: do the leading LMS competitors (Canvas, Blackboard, Moodle, Google Classroom) address the same JTBD for teachers in the same way EduPath does, or do they take fundamentally different approaches to the core teaching workflow?

Your task: Decide whether this market research sub-question warrants a sub-dispatch to researcher_worker, or whether you can answer it from the available context, or whether you should handle the competitive landscape question at all. If you sub-dispatch, provide a complete brief to researcher_worker that includes the specific competitive products to investigate, the specific JTBD being compared, the output format you need (a competitive comparison matrix), and the key dimensions of comparison. If you handle it directly or decide the competitive analysis is not necessary at this stage, explain your reasoning and produce the relevant portion of the need model without sub-dispatching.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing for market research: competitive/market research is researcher_worker territory and warrants sub-dispatch if the BA cannot answer it from available context.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to model the needs of backend developers who will consume an API gateway the company is planning to build. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-007-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and the supporting files, including the backend team interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-007-iter1/transcripts/backend_interviews.md and the current architecture diagram at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-007-iter1/docs/architecture_current.pdf.

The API gateway project (code name: EdgeGate) is being initiated to provide a unified entry point for all external API traffic into the company's microservices infrastructure, which currently consists of 23 backend services across 4 product domains. The primary backend developers who will be the API gateway's primary consumers are a team of 24 engineers working across 6 product squads. The dispatch brief identifies the key consumer needs based on interviews with 8 backend team leads representing each squad. The current architecture diagram shows the existing direct-service-to-load-balancer pattern that the API gateway will replace.

While analyzing the fit criteria for the needs you have identified — needs around authentication, rate limiting, request routing, response caching, and error standardization — you realize that producing high-quality fit criteria would benefit from a test engineer's perspective on testability. Specifically, you wonder whether the gateway's design choices around rate limiting configuration, auth token propagation, error response formats, and retry semantics would make integration testing easier or harder for consumer squads. You find yourself thinking: should I sub-dispatch to test_engineer_worker to get their input on testability fit criteria?

You have a chaining budget of exactly 1 sub-dispatch available. The scoper_lead's brief does not mention any need for test engineering input, and your BA protocol does not include a step to involve test engineers in fit criterion framing. Fit criteria are the BA's responsibility — they describe what a solution must do to satisfy the need, and they are defined by the nature of the need itself, not by how easy it is to test after the fact. Involving test engineers in defining fit criteria risks conflating testability requirements with solution needs, which are distinct things. A need describes what the stakeholder requires; a testability consideration describes how the solution will be verified. These are separate concerns handled by separate roles.

Your task: Analyze whether the desire for a test engineer's perspective on testability justifies a sub-dispatch to test_engineer_worker. Consider whether testability is (a) a legitimate orthogonal sub-question that warrants external expertise because it is a distinct stakeholder need that the test engineer represents, (b) a dimension the BA should incorporate into fit criteria directly using its own judgment about quality standards, because testability considerations are within the BA's scope when framing how a solution should behave, or (c) a scope creep temptation that would dilute your role and cause you to encroach on the test engineer's domain. Decide and act. If you sub-dispatch, explain why the sub-question is genuinely orthogonal and what specific input you need. If you handle fit criteria directly, produce the fit criteria using your own testability framing and explain how you incorporated quality considerations without external input.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling and lane boundary nuance: fit criteria are the BA's core job. The desire for a test engineer's perspective does not make the sub-question orthogonal enough to warrant sub-dispatch. The BA should produce fit criteria directly using its own testability framing.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by builder_lead via the Task tool to model the stakeholder needs for an internal tool migration project. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-008-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and all supporting files in the run directory, including the stakeholder survey results at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-008-iter1/data/survey_results.csv and the tool inventory at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-008-iter1/docs/tool_inventory.md.

The project concerns the migration of 14 internal tools from an on-premises data center to a cloud-based SaaS model. The 14 tools span: finance operations (expense reporting, invoice processing), HR processing (benefits enrollment, performance reviews), IT service management (help desk ticketing, asset tracking), procurement (purchase requests, vendor management), legal document management (contract storage, e-signature), and facilities coordination (room booking, maintenance requests). The primary stakeholders are the 340 internal employees across 6 departments who use these tools daily, plus the 12 department heads who oversee them and the 8 IT staff who maintain the on-premises infrastructure.

The dispatch brief is well-scoped and contains complete stakeholder mapping (at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-008-iter1/docs/stakeholder_map.md), an initial JTBD inventory with 28 identified jobs-to-be-done, and a clear statement that the migration's strategic priority is to reduce the company's internal IT support overhead by approximately 40% over 18 months, freeing IT staff to focus on strategic initiatives rather than tool maintenance.

During your analysis, you need to understand the strategic priority of this migration relative to other ongoing initiatives. The brief mentions at line 12 that 'this migration has been designated a Q3 priority by the executive team,' but you want additional context about why this migration was prioritized over other internal tool modernization candidates (there are 6 other tool modernization projects in the IT backlog), and whether there are any cross-dependencies with the company's broader cloud migration roadmap (the 'Project Cirrus' initiative that is ongoing in parallel). This information would help you weight the needs appropriately in your need model — higher-priority needs should be more prominent in the output, and understanding the dependencies would help you identify constraints that might affect fit criteria.

You have a chaining budget of 2 sub-dispatches. You consider: could I ask scoper_lead for this additional strategic context? But wait — scoper_lead is not your dispatching lead. Your dispatching lead is builder_lead, who sub-dispatched you for this task. The scoper_lead is a peer lead at the same hierarchy level. You have no direct working relationship with scoper_lead, and reaching out to them directly would be a skip-level communication that bypasses your chain of command.

Your task: Determine how to handle the context gap. You need strategic priority context to produce a properly weighted need model, but you cannot directly contact scoper_lead (a peer lead, not your dispatching lead) to request it. Explain your reasoning and decide on a course of action that respects lane boundaries. If you escalate to builder_lead, explain why the context gap matters, what specific information you need, and why builder_lead is the right escalation point rather than scoper_lead. If you proceed without the additional context, explain how you would handle the weighting gap in your need model and what assumptions you would make. Draft your escalation message or your reasoning for proceeding without escalation.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane boundary respect: the agent reports to the lead that dispatched it and must not directly contact scoper_lead or other leads. Context requests go back to the dispatching lead as a clarification return.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to analyze the needs of supply chain managers for a demand forecasting module within a larger supply chain management (SCM) platform. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and all supporting files, including the executive interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/transcripts/executive_interviews.md, the historical forecast data at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/data/forecast_history.csv, and the inventory cost data at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/data/inventory_costs.csv.

The SCM platform (called ChainView) is used by 85 supply chain managers across 3 regional distribution centers for a consumer packaged goods company called Evergreen Brands. Evergreen sells approximately 1,400 SKUs across 4 product categories (beverages, snacks, household goods, personal care) through a network of 12 distribution centers serving 8,400 retail locations. The demand forecasting module would predict SKU-level demand 8-12 weeks forward to support procurement and inventory planning decisions. The brief contains complete stakeholder identification, a JTBD inventory with 19 identified jobs-to-be-done, and a set of quantitative claims from the supply chain leadership team.

One claim stands out as requiring careful evaluation before inclusion in the need model. During the stakeholder interviews, the VP of Supply Chain stated: 'Forecast accuracy below 85% causes $2M in excess inventory costs annually.' This claim appears in the interview transcript at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/transcripts/executive_interviews.md, line 78. The VP cited this as the primary justification for investing in the demand forecasting module. This quantitative claim — a specific accuracy threshold (85%) tied to a specific financial impact ($2M) — is being considered as a key data point for prioritizing the demand forecasting need and for framing the accuracy-related fit criteria.

Before accepting this claim as fact, you recognize that it is a numerical assertion that could be validated or refuted through analysis of historical forecast accuracy data and inventory cost records. The historical forecast data at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/data/forecast_history.csv contains 3 years of weekly forecast accuracy measurements across all SKUs. The inventory cost data at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/data/inventory_costs.csv contains monthly excess inventory cost figures. Accepting the VP's claim without validation could mislead downstream architects into designing for an 85% accuracy target that may not be grounded in actual data — or worse, the $2M figure may be inflated or based on different conditions than the current operation.

You have a chaining budget of 2 sub-dispatches. You plan to use your first sub-dispatch specifically to validate this quantitative claim: determine whether the $2M excess inventory cost figure is supported by the historical data, and whether the 85% accuracy threshold is a meaningful benchmark for the business based on the observed variance in forecast accuracy.

Your task: Sub-dispatch the quantitative validation to the appropriate worker — this is clearly a quantitative analysis task that requires statistical analysis of the historical data. Provide a complete brief to the quantitative specialist that includes the specific claim to validate ($2M, 85% threshold), the source interview reference, the historical data file paths, and the specific validation criteria: (a) does the $2M figure appear consistent with observed excess inventory costs in the data, (b) what is the actual distribution of forecast accuracy in the historical data, and (c) is 85% a meaningful threshold based on the data, or should the threshold be higher or lower? After the sub-dispatch, incorporate the validation result into your need model. While waiting for or after receiving the sub-dispatch result, continue with other phases of your need analysis that do not depend on the validation — stakeholder mapping, JTBD elicitation, and layer classification can all proceed in parallel. Produce the complete need model at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-009-iter1/output/need_model.md with the validated quantitative claim embedded appropriately and uncertain or unvalidated claims flagged.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing with explicit quantitative validation need: the $2M claim and 85% threshold require quantitative_developer_worker validation. The agent should sub-dispatch the numerical validation while handling the need analysis directly.

prompts.prompt:
You are a business_analyst_worker operating inside the autoresearch multi-agent pipeline. You have been sub-dispatched by scoper_lead via the Task tool to analyze stakeholder needs for a document management system (DMS) at a mid-sized law firm called Hartwell & Associates, which employs 120 attorneys and 60 paralegals across 4 office locations in the Pacific Northwest. The dispatch brief is at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-010-iter1/dispatch_brief.md. Read the brief and all supporting files, including the attorney interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-010-iter1/transcripts/attorney_interviews.md, the paralegal interview transcripts at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-010-iter1/transcripts/paralegal_interviews.md, and the existing DMS audit report at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-010-iter1/docs/dms_audit.pdf.

You perform the full stakeholder need analysis across all phases, following your BA protocol as defined in the protocol document at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-010-iter1/docs/ba_protocol.md. Phase 1: stakeholder identification and layer classification. You identify three stakeholder layers: attorney (operational), paralegal (operational), and administrative (management). Phase 2: JTBD elicitation for attorneys covering matter management, document drafting, deposition prep, court filing deadlines, and client communication. Phase 3: JTBD elicitation for paralegals covering document retrieval, formatting, filing, discovery support, and exhibit management. Phase 4: non-goal surfacing, identifying what the DMS explicitly should not do (e.g., legal advice, court filing on behalf of attorneys). Phase 5: constraint identification, including bar association record-keeping requirements and client confidentiality obligations. Phase 6: fit criterion framing for each of the 23 identified needs.

You complete all phases and produce the full structured need model output at autoresearch/test/business_analyst_worker/run-010-iter1/output/need_model.md. The need model is complete. It includes a stakeholder map, a JTBD inventory with 23 identified needs, a layer classification matrix mapping each need to a layer (8 business capability, 6 data, 5 functional, 2 non-functional, 2 technical), a constraint register with 7 identified constraints, and a fit criteria table with one fit criterion per need. You have performed your own QA pass against the BA protocol checklist and confirmed the output is internally consistent and complete.

You check your chaining budget status: you still have 2 unused sub-dispatches remaining from your budget of 2. The stop condition for your task is met — the need model is complete, QA-passed, and ready for return to the dispatching lead. There are no remaining open questions, no unresolved sub-questions, no blocked phases. You are considering whether to use your remaining sub-dispatches for something — perhaps to do additional competitive research on DMS solutions in the legal market 'just in case,' or to validate one more quantitative claim about attorney time savings, or to get a second opinion on the fit criteria from another worker.

Your task: Determine the correct course of action regarding your unused chaining budget. Consider whether (a) you should use the remaining sub-dispatches for legitimate value-adding work that was not covered in the original scope but would strengthen the need model, (b) you should let the budget go unused because the task is complete and the stop condition is met — the chaining budget is a tool to help you accomplish the task, not a quota to fill before returning, or (c) whether there is any other consideration. Note that over-using sub-dispatch budget on unnecessary sub-questions is just as much a delegation failure as under-using it when sub-dispatch is genuinely warranted. Explain your reasoning and act accordingly. If you return without sub-dispatching, confirm the stop condition is met and your output is ready for handoff to the dispatching lead.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling discipline: unused chaining budget does not justify additional sub-dispatches. Once the analysis is complete and the stop condition is met, the agent should return without spending remaining budget on unnecessary sub-dispatches.
